reviews.review_text: The game was incredibly promising two years ago at release and the bones are still there. It has a foundation that no other medieval city (village) building game has, and I really hope for a future where I can change this review. However, for the past two years we've gotten very little content (basically just stone castles that are frankly, not all that interesting) and just rebalance after rebalance that haven't actually changed how the game feels. Every "major" update has me saying cool while reading patchnotes, and quitting again after an hour because the "improved balance" doesn't improve the fun in any meaningful way. The communication from dev(s) has steadily dropped off to the point that the CEO of the publisher stepped in, which was promising at first but has quickly also turned lackluster because it seems there's nothing to actually report from the devs. A year ago I excused the lack of content as a responsible dev (or team, it's really unclear how many people are actually working on this game) trying to lay a stronger foundation to make integrating future content easier. However, with all the "major" reworks doing basically nothing, or undoing previous major reworks, it seems to me that the dev doesn't actually know what he wants this game to be, and is chasing himself in a circle with no direction - or the worse option, which is just doing nothing. Until I see some kind of meaningful accountability for the slow progress (besides the occasional non-apologetic tweet about game development being hard) and a good explanation from the dev for why the rebalances actually matter and should be done, I cannot recommend spending money on this game.

I wish I could rate this up because it has a great foundation for a medieval city builder and was a joy at first. It is also stunningly beautiful.

It has been in early access for over two years now with very minimal improvements. Trade is still pretty incomplete and farming is a micromanaging chore switching labor between seasons. Farming is essential to growing actual towns out of villages so there is no opting out of this micromanagement. In addition the use of families as population units is frustrating because it becomes impossible to keep track of how far away each family lives from their workplace once you have more than 10 or so in your town. This leads to problems where you have villagers spending most of the working day walking to work. These quality of life problems haven't been fixed for a very long time now and has caused me to stop playing. 

Trade is inconsistent and random. You can not reliably import a good into your town that you can not produce. Usually the volume is too low to really support your town. In the event you do manage to get enough volume to meaningfully supply your town, the price quickly skyrockets and drains your regional wealth.

If this game moved to a full release stage I would gladly change my review. In its current state I am entirely skeptical we ever get a full release. The latest updates have not done anything to bring me back into the game, and the latest developer blog updates seem to suggest they are still going back and forth on a lot of core game mechanics like perks and trade which is really frustrating as it has been in early access for 2 years now and they are asking for $40 right now. Insane price point in my opinion.

TLDR; Game is very incomplete and has remained this way for 2 years and not shown many signs of improving. New AI and castle stone walls are cool but to prioritize this over core game mechanics and quality of life improvements is disappointing. There are many mechanics of the game implemented inconsistently and some not at all(perks). Charging $40 for this in its current state is pretty shameful.

The most recent update fixed all of the performance related issues I was having and I am back to enjoying the game the way I used to. I am VERY HAPPY with the updates to the engine and core systems. I know that you are receiving many negative reviews from people who are complaining about slow release of game features but please know that experienced gamers like myself are impressed that you have put fundamentals first and are choosing to optimize first before just rolling out new content that isn't scaleable.

The improvements to the resource systems have made developing towns more intuitive. You now need to engage with all of the core mechanics in order to succeed broadly, and those systems are no longer frustrating to engage with. The updates to customizing production limits and brackets for trade and industry and specialists has put an end to the pausing and unpausing of extensions. All very good.

Suggestions:

Automatic crop rotation is still not quite ideal as it does not allow you to pre-plow fallow fields earlier in the season prior to harvest and planting. With such limited time to harvest and plant it is important to be able to plow fallow fields in spring without having to disable the crop rotation automation. Setting worker areas for farms should work like drawing a boundary, not like a circle that gets bigger or smaller. I still feel like I spend an inordinate amount of time microing farmhouse allocations and field designations.

Castle planner is still clunky and because it is high-stakes resource wise, it can be extremely frustrating when something goes wonky. 

BIGGEST SUGGESTION:

Let people customize resource starts during game creation. I know you want people to adapt to whatever they're given, but I don't always want to play the hand I'm dealt for hours upon hours. Sometimes I just really want a region with fertile fields and a quarry so I can build pretty castles and farms. I shouldn't have to restart and regenerate maps over and over to play the kind of game I want. Let people curate the kind of experience they want to have.

Keep up the great work. Awesome game.

The Star Citizen of city builders. Looked complete enough to get a LOT of people to pay for it - now that it's made its money, the game won't be finished. 

What is getting added is low quality. Clearly no more fuel in the tank with these guys, don't get scammed.

This game is unlike any other city builder I've ever played and will likely ever play again. It's everything I wanted out of Banished way back in the day and has a bright and promising future ahead of it.

I love this game.

I cannot recommend buying it at its current development cycle. I remain hopeful for future updates and I am more than willing to be patient with the developer to continue to polish this gem.

I've been checking back with this game since I bought it on release. Every time I'm dissapointed with the lack of real updates. I enjoyed the game for what it is, but i'm starting to get disillusioned, and worry the devs don't have the incentive or committment to finish the game. It's a shame. I hope they prove me wrong, in which case I will change this review.

I had high hopes for this game, but trying to play it again fir the first time in 2 years I noticed there has been almost zero development. Amost nothing has changed, no new features and no gameplay improvements. A real waste of potential.

The games foundational building aspects for the timeline it falls in is second to none in my opinion. Very detailed, graphics are exceptional. The basis of reality is open for all to see as food becomes the foundation of all, it is based on the real factual obstacles of the time period and plays out nicely in the game. 
There are some drawbacks however, but these drawbacks and obstacles must be viewed in the context of the game still being in development, or an ongoing development.

First of all, you can only go so far in the game in all but the peaceful build mode. In other words, anytime you put the opposing Lord off map in the game, you've lost the game from the very start and never see the developments unfold in their entirety. After playing hundreds of hours, trying countless strategies to fend off the opposing lords eventual claim to your land, you simply cannot win. The primary reason is 2 fold, first, they claim your land so quickly you don't have the time to trade for the necessary weapons amount to defeat them and second, you don't have the time to build the necessary weapons production in your land to fend them off. This leads to the overwhelming strength of the opposing lord always victorious. I have yet to see past level 2 town, and even then, not for a significant time in level 2 to make the outcome change. And as I've said, I have tried many many different ways to come at this issue. If you spend your retinue for mercanaries, you fall in to debt so heavy with the kings taxes you never recover.
Can it be changed? A more reasonable opposing lord force size? A more measured time line of their appearance to allow proper preparation for the eventual claiming of your land? That is up to the developer, but, I've decided to shelve this game until these problems are addressed to accommodate game play that is feasible.

I will revisit the info on updates and follow any future developments that help foster game play that allows for reasonable solutions to this particular issue. Until then I wish the developer well and hope they continue to build a product suitable for reasoned game play. As of now, I have to give it a thumbs down, but with reserved optimism it can be turned in to a thumbs up.

Played for 3 years and almost nothing has changed. The NPC AI is completely broken and brain-dead. Workers constantly ignore assigned tasks; my plank factories just sit idle despite having plenty of wood. Construction is painfully slow because the AI prefers doing random personal stuff over working. Speeding up the game changes nothing. Early Access is just an excuse for awful logic bugs that are never fixed. Total waste of time.

I loved the time I've spent playing this game so far, but after beating the AI in my world for the first time, I feel like there is not much more to the game anymore.

The biggest issue is that there are not many things to develop, and once you figure out the best way to do things there is no reason to try anything else because other methods are just that bad. Don't bother building farms for food, the yield is far too low for the resources and workers to be worth it. Need anything in a region that it can't produce itself? Just gather the infinite resources from a crowned mine and trade for them, no big deal. Sending resources from region to region cannot be tweaked or finetuned, so don't bother. Brewing beer, or even building a castle seem completely useless. Honestly, outside of figuring out food, fuel and weapons, there is nothing else to do

- 05/14/26 -
Manor Lords is everything I want in a game, and on the cusp of being more than that. If you enjoy games such as Anno, Total War, Cities Skylines, and of course enjoy a challenge in a time-accurate medieval setting... it takes features from all of those somehow. This scratches the itch I've had for a large-scale city-building game, but with the deeper level management of a colony sim. If that grabs your attention, then continue reading, if not then maybe this isn't for you. I recommend you look into the older Anno games or the RimWorld-esque Going Medieval. Of course I have things I want, like any game that is in early access and still able to be changed. Most of this involves efficiency and QOL options, but also half-baked features that would greatly expand the late game. 

The meat and taters. Manor Lords is committed to the detail that the setting deserves. The game relies on several simple systems that each have their own depth. You can choose the level of micromanaging you want, and it increases in difficulty with the complexity of your settlement. The graphics are beautiful, the game is built in Unreal. Performance may vary, I run the game at 60 into late game. My specs are attached and it uses a uniform level of resources, so take that as you will. You can and will spend time at eye level view watching villagers perform their tasks (with unexpected detail). The villagers have voice lines, the pool is small, but appreciated; "Does that joint look crooked?". The soundscape is vast, birds will chirp, sheep will bahhh, and bees will buzz. This plays into the music. It is slow and atmospheric; then Lambert gets jiggy with it on the flute. Buildings are built piece by piece. There is an icon to show you the progress, but seeing a house fully framed is a good indicator. Each day is simulated, the seasons affect everything. A year of in-game time can take hours. The early game is mastered (1-3 years) and only becomes difficult in the middle. You drastically scale after having a stable food supply, and suddenly you have 30 jobs that you need done, 15 people to do them, and only 5 that are actually available. This is fun.

You can feel your settlement grow, and you will always have something to do. In reality, there are few buildings for you to build. This is a feature, and you would lose your mind if every little thing needed a separate shack. This game works by assigning families to production buildings (mines, artisans, etc...) which you need unassigned families to build, this is the loop. These families consist of 3 people who contribute to their assigned task. Each family requires several goods, which increase in variety as you upgrade them similar to the Anno games. This allows you to scale at your own pace. If all of your needs are met you don't have to grow, vice versa. There are ways to meet needs with trade (still very necessary, gotta get the $$), but for simplicity just assume you will always want to be self sufficient.

However, this can lead to a justified sense that your families are inefficient. See, their homes have optional but necessary 'extensions' like workshops (artisans) or having a garden or livestock. They are additional work requirements on your family, who still have their OTHER job. The real kick to the head is that families cannot be chosen for a task without a lot of tedious clicking and reassigning. Your gardener that has his own plot of farmable land will also be working in your fields. Three people stretched between their backyard carrots and a 2 acre farm. You'll see a family walking halfway across the map to their job just to turn around to go rest. A perk exists that makes it so they only work their gardens, but it is worse than the other options so I don't use it. You might and I accept that as a decision to make. This is somewhat helped by having multiple families living on the same plot of land. The number increases as you upgrade their house(s), remember "Quantity has a quality all its own". You can out-scale your efficiency problems, but still be frustrated when winter is coming and your farmer is busy walking back to his house 3 miles away to rest; and that's IF you can scale. This is my biggest frustration with the game. I have my fixes which would obviously be the best... lol

The following are framed as 'I want...' because in any game there is intentional levels of tedium. You control everything and if it was done for you, are you even really playing it? A game shouldn't punish you for being successful, and Manor Lords doesn't, but it can feel like it.        

- I want my families to be a max of 5 people, but start with 3, and let them marry and have children. Sheep can do it, so can we. That is organic growth (monthly growth is capped and tied to satisfaction) and delays the boost for a long period, let's say 14 years (they yearn for the mines). The chance of children can be tied to satisfaction, needs, space...

- I want my extra families on a plot to be optionally assigned to the extension. This is how it works with level 1 extensions (gardens and livestock), but level 2 extensions (artisans) turn a home into a workshop that every family on that plot of land works. This leads to a level 3 home with an extra house (3 pop) working at a place that functions with just 1... 3 families is enough to fully staff a logging camp. Please let me decide that. I'm fine with 1 family that makes sense, but please. 

- I want an auto assign button. This is a simple one (but I avoid writing code at all costs), I just want to be able to assign the closest families to a task without the clicking.

- I want seasonal jobs to be a full feature, not something you figure out by clicking all the buttons at least once. You can pause production and have your families returned to the unassigned pool and reassigned when you resume production, but it feels janky. I'm not planting crops all year so I don't need 10 families assigned to farms year round. This is historical and I think it would be a natural addition. Given that you CAN do it currently, I'm not whining about it. 

The above are my biggest wants. Everything else really just seems to not be fully implemented yet.

- Pathfinding is bad, but that seems to be one of the developer's biggest priorities, so womp womp.

- Combat exists? It is limited to raiders and bandits, and an optional off-map baron that claims all the land. You can form militias and there are a variety of units recognizable to TW players; but don't expect a large war... yet. 

- AI players suck. You will not have any real competition and can stomp them any time if you invest in your forces at all. They won't attack you and they will stagnate within 5 years. You can conquer their regions, counter their claims, but it just falls flat. 

- Castles look pretty but are pointless. You will not be making a walled-in town, you literally can't. The castle builder is buggy, it will freeze and crash your game. They should be the ultimate ego boost, an utter waste of resources, and what you rename your region after. 

To wrap this up, if I didn't mention how good or how bad a feature is, then I'm either content with how it's implemented or haven't had problems with it. The important thing with any early access game, or in my opinion gem, is trust. I trust the vision for this game, and I trust Hooded Horse to keep to it. Like I said, the graphics are beautiful and the atmosphere and music are so good that my partner watches me play just to listen to it. This game is a future classic, and I expect that many developers will emulate it. I love this game, and my criticisms come from a place of wanting it to succeed in every way.

Currently, this game is a well-earned 7/10. This is an ambitious project, and ambition takes time. To the developer, thank you for giving this game the effort it deserves, and I cannot wait to see what's in store for the future. Almost at 8000 characters, oh my...

The mechanics and gameplay here are solid -- I consider this one of the better city builders in my library based on how intuitive the system is. However, after 7 years of early access... and especially as a developer myself -- why are devs still refining the pathfinding and balance? There is no decent upgrading past houses/artisanship, and the castle mechanics are completely empty. That part was extremely disappointing solely given how long this game has been out. 

If you are okay with a game that likely will never be in its full state, go for it when it's on sale, but I cannot recommend the full price for a game missing so much functionality. This game will be amazing if the developers finish it.

This game has a lot of potential, but it seems development stalled. If it had more content the replay value would be much higher, right now it is an unfinished mess with nothing to do once you have a stable settlement running. It lacks content to beautify your settlement, has no mod support, and patches are very rare and mostly aimed at altering game mechanics instead of adding actual content to the game. 

The game is beautiful, but shallow. Everything feels unfinished. Yes, it is in early access, but it feels the (small) dev team does not really care about communicating what their plans are, The patches lack substance and are too far between.  

I would only buy it on a big discount. Expect an unfinished game that feels more like a tech demo. A few weeks of playtime before you've seen everything and run out of things to do because this game lacks content to customise the look of your settlement. 

I kept on playing this game over and over, trying to understand what I was missing out on, why everyone was so hyped about it. It feels like I've wasted too much time on it. Once you have the feeling you've seen it all, you've seen it all.
i like the mechanics of the game and graphics are great and i like chill games

Interesting 4x game, focusing most on city building and resource management, with some fighting.
If you know Settlers 3, this is basicly an advanced version, but with tons better grapics.
Plenty of eye candy setting adjustability, so old PC's can run the game as well as push the most modern PC's to the edge as well, if going 2.5k or 4K resolution.
Needs lots of RAM and CPU power as well, as your population grows bigger.
Game is still en BETA and pre-release mode, so lots of development to be done still.

[h1] Scroll to bottom for TLDR. [/h1] (I've even used numbers to help ;D

[h3] Graphics [/h3]

ML looks glamorous visually from a isometric or 3rd person view albeit with some clunky animations around the villagers and troops. Whilst the theme of the aesthetics coalesce to create something that looks nice and immersive, and in this type of game it helps, it isn't everything. Troop engagement can feel a bit wonky as collision between units feels a bit off and the combat is reminiscent of what I imagine battles in the settings period would look like (I'm old but not that old) and it's nice to have a sim game that has the option to actually have battles amongst your lovingly built towns. Although this feature is available in other similar games most the time I find it to be point / click of a friendly unit to an enemy and watch as a little symbol appears denoting who's winning the engagement.

[h3] Gameplay [/h3]

The core game play loop in ML is the growing and prospering of your village, growing into a town, large town, ect. If your familiar with other city builders then this is pretty boiler plate stuff. It does this kinda competently, as in the basics are there, and they are enjoyable, however some feel skin deep at certain points. The growing of crops is handled in a fun way, with soil fertility, crop rotation, seasons and harvesting times in the mix, however it still feels that the payoff is somewhat lacking such as the soil fertility never seems to recuperate as intended, or the fact that one region on a map will have RICH soil fertility which is usually needed for growing hops, which is turned into ale (the life blood of any bohemian city) which then leads into one hell of a bottle neck. Although there are work a rounds currently like importing the stuff or farming at reduced yields it kinda feels that if these systems were working as intended (I assume) then a lot of these weird issues would disappear and make the sim part of this game feel infinitely more rewarding once the player pulls them off and there established. The mustering of arms and mining of materials is a fun little loop as it works as intended, and is usable for fielding your militias which is a nice pay off as you visually seeing your efforts. Gathering of food is good albeit I feel every sizeable forest should have a output of hunting game (even if it's small game like rabbits, compared to the deer currently available) and if said forest is deforested then you run the risk of losing the animals habitat and they disperse. Currently you can migrate the wild animals which is nice for relocating them closer or somewhere more convenient to your town. The combat is reminiscent of some grand strategy games and is at least a feature. It would suck, if v1.0 releases, to build your fancy looking castle to have it be just that. A fancy looking set dressing. At least with combat as a option one day we may get to finally mount our cities defences, and if that isn't your cup of tea then the options there to turn it off. Overall I think these systems work on a foundational level but I hope they continue to work on them to improve the meaningful impact they have as the player enters the mid to late games. 
 
[h3] Audio [/h3]

The music is well composed, and fit the game perfectly. Clearly effort has gone into it and the vocals are suitably chorus, reminiscent of church stuff I think (I've never been). Hopefully they add more upon release as a 1hr 44min loop for a game where potential play sessions may fall in the 8-10 hour could get repetitive, but a 1-2 hour session you probably wouldn't notice, or just stick on a music playlist or something. Good job Elben Schutte, Daniel Caleb, RSVP Voices and Pressure Cooker.

[h3] PC Requirements [/h3]

Meh could probably get away with running it on a dated machine nowadays, on low to medium settings with minimal issues. Wouldn't look as good as the promotional videos but at least you'd be playing the game. Otherwise I've run into 1 crash over 30-ish hours and it was a menu conflict I think that caused the game to seize up, so I'll probably avoid doing that again for now. Disk space wise it's not diabolical, I think less than 30GB so not likely a game I'll have to think about deleting to make room for others.

[h3] Story / Difficulty / Time [/h3]

Story wise it's a open sandbox city sim, yeah you can defeat the on map opponents or continually bang heads with the off map opponent but other than that I suppose create your own, or don't. I've no idea if a campaign is planned or if more is planned on the regional politics front as that would make for more immersive and varied play through. Difficulty is cope able, settings can be tweaked to make the AI more passive or left to it's own devices it can be left to run riot with yearly rampages into your regions. This can get tiresome as currently other than defence with arms there's no other option to deal with them, but if you enjoy the combat then it's not a bad thing. If sieges become a thing then the battles will only become more enjoyable imo. If you gel with these sorts of games then you could spend 500 - 600 hours playing, but as is I'd say 60 - 100 hours to give yourself enough runs to try out the different features and beat the AI a couple of times in there, whilst failing a lot more to some bandits you forgot were coming at the end of year 2 or 3. 

[h3] Price / Bugs / Updates [/h3]

If you made it this far have a cookie.
The price is pretty high for a early access but if it pays off with a full release and I get a hundred hours out of it then I'm good. I had the money at the time of purchase and Slavic Magic seems like a good dude judging by the discord, I do however regret more of the times I've spent £80 on a night out to only be left with hangxiety, than EA games, and none of them have ever caused me to spend the next day throwing up. I've only encountered graphical glitches and a few bugs with the tech tree (ie it's none implementation) or systems not functioning 100% and the 1 seize up I mentioned. Nothing has come up to cause a game breaking experience or made me quit in a exasperated sigh. Updates are coming out, although I don't seem to see a public schedule and there somewhat superficial sometimes. They've released a beta branch, for a game in it's beta, so there's a stable version and then the more regularly updated beta version of the beta... either way I would have liked more to have been added since release to the public but if going from a team of 1 to any greater number has taught me you have to spend a sizeable amount of time getting everyone up to speed that the payoff usually comes years down the line (in regards to game development, other things like a McDonalds it pretty quick)  

[h3] TLDR [/h3]

Graphics - 8/10
Gameplay - 7/10
Audio - 9/10
PC Requirements- 9/10
Story / Difficulty / Time - 8/10
Price / Bugs / Updates - 7/10

Would I buy again - Yeah, I hope Slavic Magic continues to update and release the game, if not I've made worse financial decisions.

Should you buy it - If you are comfortable taking a small risk and seeing where it goes, go ahead, if your swept up in any form of hype then probably best to put it on a wishlist and continue on with your day, you ain't missing the boat to be left on shore watching other people sail off into the sunset. I think that was the Titanic's situation and look what happened there.

At this point it really seems like the developer has a ton of money from EA sales and has now abandoned the game for intents and purposes. Don’t recommend picking this up unless there starts to be updates again

This is my first game to actually not recommend. for a developer working for SEVEN years with a team joining on later, its just unacceptable. in my first 30 minutes, I was softlocked because when I unassigned a family they were gone but still took houses up (happened thrice now) and most of the content says "coming soon" even if its as simple as some claiming land buttons or literal perks (which somehow take them years to make??) 

this is a scam.
The Dev is incredibly slow and if this gaming hasn't meaningfully been updated sense launch.
I will change my review if the game starts actually getting meaningful updates, but development is way too slow.

It's fine. Very slow. Has potential but you are greeted with an opening passage that says it's already been in development for 7 years, and there's no release date in sight. Not a huge amount of content. Other city builders are better for your money currently, but add this to a wishlist to see if it ever releases.

I hope to be able to change this review in the future, but they're just charging way too much for the current state of development this game is in. This is certainly beta and not even early access, but you're still paying the price of a full, completed game.

alot of these reviews are positive but for 40 bucks, for a game without a single mech. complete, everything is locked under early access. but if ur paying 40 bucks there should at least be more than one way to play the game but as soon as you have a good idea you realize the game isnt built out enough to complete said idea , fun city builder but only cosmetically

How is this game getting worse with updates. Things that worked before, no longer do. 
Butchers take sheep and do nothing. They butcher 3 or 4 and you have to save/load to get them moving again. Barley, malt and ale all disappear from when loading a game. Meaning you have to wait for a harvest, then malt, and then ale for the tavern to work each time you play.
Fields DO NOT regain fertility with fallow fields. Crop rotation does nothing, leave it fallow for 3 years? Nope, still 8% fertility.
Large pasture can only conain for example 50 sheep but if you make 10 tiny pasture with same space you can have 400??
 
Fish are pointless. They are only good very early game and then pointless to ever use again. Need either ocean access for larger fish pop or just up the pop in existing rivers/ponds.

Small Game area(Red animal hunting area). Just remove it and have the hunting camp production be based on your tiles wilderness rating.

Firewood from market is to slow. i had 2 fully manned storages(12 workers)  and 3 markets but a town of 30 houses freezes because the markets cant stock fast enough. Add a House insulation upgrade so you can upgrade houses and decrease them needing infinite firewood/coal.

I had this game for quite some time. There were a few improvements like the Castle. Barely an upgrade, as i cant exactly call it a castle under the most generous circumstances. Buts its also a pointless unused mechanic unless you take 20 in game years to build a wall around your entire town. The Core city mechanics need attention first.

Eh, it was enjoyable at first because it kind of had this new car smell; new things that other city builders didn't have, but as time went on and the updates slowed to a trickle, it lost that smell rather quickly.  I do understand that it has a solo dev (that now has a team, btw), but to be honest, it's really just another game stuck in EA purgatory.  It eventually became boring after a certain point because it lacks any real substance in the end game.  Now granted, I haven't played it again with the last couple of update but from what I've seen, it wouldn't really change my opinion of it.

My biggest gripes:

1) The farming was still broken when I played it.  Seems rather weird that the most important part of feudal life was, in fact, farming.
2) Combat is still janky.  There are no real tactics like in Total War.  I mean, you can encircle your enemy and that may work, but I mean, why would the enemy try to push across a bridge to face my grouped up militia when they are outnumbered?  I'm not the greatest strategist alive by any means, but I can see where that's a farce and it's only one of many.  And why does it cost so much to raise soldiers temporarily?  Do you not realize that it was considered a civic duty, an honorable one, to defend your lord's town?  Temporary defense forces, armies, etc. were raised constantly that either costed just food or sometimes, they weren't paid or compensated at all.  Town guard and permanent troops were paid and fed.  
3) Micromanagement.  Granted, city builders rely on some micromanagement to truly keep you engaged.  Some.  You have to do everything.  Even as your city is at its max.  I mean, throughout history there were bailiffs, etc.  There is no real AI for any decision making.
4) Why can I not see any progression with numbers or graphs?  If it's realism you're after, give us a scribe so we can see what's actually going on.
5) Economy.  The supply of an import can't be so low in other 'regions' that the price goes up that high.  That doesn't make sense at all.  Highly unlikely that any city would export certain goods if they were that scarce.  Trade is so bad in general, which sucks, because trade between cities was an integral part to successful towns in medieval Europe.  Everything is so expensive as well, which granted, it would have been back then, but, I mean, it's so expensive to do ANYTHING.
6) It's impossible to keep up with families after a certain point.  
7) I do understand that they have a small team but there's really no excuse to be actively making money from this game and it take this long to fix all the problems.  But, that is the reality to modern gaming in my opinion.  The tools to make games are so readily available that anyone can start and developers don't realize that while constant updates aren't expected, they are expected to happen in a timely fashion and simply can't meet the demand.  Early Access Purgatory.  I could go as far as to say that this seems rather like a scam more so than a serious developer, and since the heat from release has died down, there's no point in hiring even more people because you've alienated all those that were willing to wait that no one will really care if it suddenly changes.  The time to do that should have been at the beginning when your initial investment would have paid off dividends.

My few pros:
1) When things go wrong, they tend to go very wrong.  This may annoy people but I like that your city can go horribly wrong.  I personally would recommend changing the wells to become foul randomly, causing disease to spread rapidly, or perhaps, fouled grain randomly because it rained too much or something.  Even a blacksmith causing half the city to go up in flames.  Those kind of things did happen and still even happens today in some rural parts of the world.  
2) The cities look really good after they come alive, good enough that I would love to walk through myself.  I feel like there should be more manure, etc. something that most games miss because animals and people just threw it out.  These cities smelled terrible.  
3) The armor looks pretty cool.  If the combat was any good, I would be enthralled.

All in all, I would give this a hard NO for right now.  Save your money for a serious developer until they show that they can stick with this and do things the right way (even from a non-developer, I can see this is poorly managed).  If it goes on sale for 5 or 10 dollars, go for it.  If it suddenly awakens and updates begin rolling, go for it.  Otherwise, run away.  I wish I could get a refund.

There are so many good things about this game, but at the end of the day I paid $40 for a game that feels very unfinished. After playing about 20 hours, I don't feel like playing more until the bugs are fixed and some more content has been added. This early access at that price point is pretty steep. A few of the bugs/annoyances I encountered:

- Villagers won't use bridges - this becomes exceptionally game-breaking when you only have a single food resource in the area and it's across a river that requires a bridge to harvest it. 
- Too few resources in regions - I've played a ton of city builders/simulations like this (the most similar is Farthest Frontier) and there are many resources in even the toughest regions that makes gameplay a challenge but not completely hopeless. Especially for food. I find it a bit unrealistic and unnecessary for an entire region of forest NOT to have animals you can harvest from a hunting camp. Prioritizing food should be #1 but the game makes it impossible to have more than 1 source of food at any time (depending on seasonality) which can seriously bottleneck growth or even make it so you're required to start in a new region. 
- Farming mechanic is broken - Fertility and yield in the game seem to be broken. Planting and sowing in a fertile region seems to have little impact on the yield at all. My field fertility went from 100% to 0% in just 2 years, even though I was leaving the field farrow for 2/3 years like the tutorial recommended. I maximized bonuses, used oxen to plow, and had the maximum amount of works for the size of the farm but still only yielded about 20 wheat for a massive piece of land that took almost 2-3 years to get fully functional. Farming in the game simply requires too much effort and resources to be worth it at this point, but then you have to pray you get a good source of food from a node like the point above. 
- The above points with regards to expanding your town into a large city are almost impossible unless you're massively micromanaging every aspect of your town, and even then the outcome isn't guaranteed. I like the concept of the houses requiring basic necessities, and a range of resources from the market before you can upgrade them but which the challenges I described (particularly with farming), I haven't found it to be possible to manage growing to a large town of level 3 houses. 

Now for the pros that will make me come back to the game in the future and I HOPE the above gets fixed soon:
- The organic framing of the house plots, fields, and roads is really great. One of my biggest peeves in city builders is that everything exists on squares or fixed shapes so your city always ends up looking the same. The layout takes a more natural approach and you can make your roads flow between buildings and work with the land a lot better. It ends up making your town look a lot less computer generated, which I really appreciate.
- The houses produce their own food/goods. I love this feature because it makes it easier to scale as your town grows, or a quick source of food early-game. I definitely found myself relying on this a LOT since the food nodes for fishing, hunting and foraging were simply not meeting the needs of my town even early on. 
- Region expansion - I definitely love the concept of building a capitol and expanding further to conquer new/different regions, especially if that region has a resource you want badly. I only wish there was an easier way to support the new settlement using resources from the established city. In some cases, I settled my new town just down the road from the existing one and yet I couldn't easily send resources to help them out beyond just the starting resources you get. For example, the pack trader (whatever it's called) MUST have an exchange of goods and cannot be one-sided. It would be nice if the large town could supply the settlement even if it was temporary. 
- The audio ambiance is not annoying or super repetitive. One thing I notice about city builders is the ambient audio always has one or two super annoying noises that happen way too frequently. Enough that I turn off the ambience all together. I actually enjoy the audio in this.

So, that's what my thoughts are so far. I don't recommend this game for the price point and lack of content/bugs. However, if you can get it on a good sale, I'd say it's probably worth about $10-15. If the bugs get fixed and more content gets added, then I could see this being worth the full $40 eventually.

I really wanted Manor Lords to become the medieval city-builder I had been waiting for, but after playing Farthest Frontier, I honestly wish I could refund this unfinished promise.

For comparison: Farthest Frontier entered Early Access in August 2022 and reached 1.0 in October 2025. During that time it became a real, playable, mechanically rich city-builder. It has farming depth, crop rotation, disease, seasons, logistics, production chains, raider pressure, policies, long-term progression, and an actual reason to keep building. Most importantly, when I play it, I get the city-builder enjoyment I was looking for. The difference is that Farthest Frontier felt like an Early Access game with a working core and rough edges to polish. Manor Lords still feels like a beautiful foundation waiting for many of its core systems to actually arrive.

Manor Lords entered Early Access in April 2024 with massive hype, beautiful screenshots, and a great medieval atmosphere. But the actual playable depth is still nowhere near where it should be. After the first few hours, the game feels more like a medieval village painter than a deep city-builder. So many systems still feel unfinished, missing, temporarily removed, or waiting for yet another “systems rework”.

Development perks, policies, mid-game depth, late-game goals, diplomacy, AI pressure, proper economic depth, meaningful regional development — all of this still feels like it is coming “later”.

The problem is not the absence of patch notes. The problem is that most of those patches did not add the deep city-builder systems I was waiting for.

Update 3 on September 26, 2024 was the last update that felt like a meaningful content step to me, adding fishing, pigs, butchers, food spoilage, crossbows and production limits. Update 4 on January 10, 2025 added bridges, two maps, a marketplace rework and a tavern upgrade. That is not nothing, but it did not meaningfully solve the core problem: Manor Lords still lacked deep city-builder progression, policies, diplomacy, AI pressure, proper economic depth, meaningful regional development and a satisfying late game.

After that, the January follow-up patches were mostly beta changes, bugfixes, hotfixes and minor adjustments. Then players waited roughly nine months for another substantial playable update, while most of the communication kept pointing to beta branches, overhauls, reworks and future systems.

The developer apologized and said he was rethinking what went wrong so this kind of delay would not happen again. But from a player’s perspective, that promise has not been convincing. Even after the later core-systems rework, the live game still feels like a beautiful foundation waiting for many of its actual city-builder systems to become complete.

This is why the communication now feels misleading to me. Players were asked to keep trusting the process, keep waiting, and accept more promises — yet the live game still does not deliver the depth that was implied by the original hype.

A patch note is not gameplay depth. A hotfix is not a completed city-builder. A beta branch is not a finished game. A “systems rework” post is not a reason to keep waiting forever.

I wanted a serious medieval city-builder. Instead, I feel like I paid for a beautiful foundation that is still waiting for most of its actual game systems to become complete.

I do not hate Early Access. I played Farthest Frontier during Early Access and had a much better time because the game kept growing into something complete. Manor Lords, on the other hand, still feels like it sold the dream first and is trying to build the actual game years later.

If you want beautiful medieval screenshots, Manor Lords can impress you for a few hours.

If you want a serious city-builder with real systems and long-term gameplay, Farthest Frontier is far ahead right now.

My advice to new players: do not buy Manor Lords based on hype, trailers, screenshots or future promises. Wait until the actual content is in the live game. Until this game is much closer to finished, I cannot recommend paying for unfinished potential, “systems rework” posts, and another round of promises.

Permanently "early access" and in the end, a fiasco.  It seems so close to being a great game, but it's a mirage.  I will never get this time back.  Honestly, this whole early access scam has run its course.  Enough already. You cannot be early access, selling a half made game, for years on end.  Sick of this.  I am not your investor.

I want to recommend this game so much, but I just can't! (at least not in its current state) 
first off, I have had too many problems with performance and my computer struggling to keep up, even with low graphical settings. Gameplay is *so* promising, and I absolutely love the mechanics of farming. But... it all falls apart when you expand your city into the new unlockable areas. I have followed countless online guides and tried to figure things out on my own even more, but its still impossible to understand the fighting and defensive army mechanics. Its also impossible to streamline production and keep an eye on trading unless you pause often. 

But with all my gripes, I still kept playing! And that's because I have so much faith in this project. I really do think this can be one of the best city builders when its developed more!!
Its an Ok game and I was hooked at first but it lost its luster pretty quickly. Basically if you start with Iron you will win and if you dont just reseed and start over...

It's so broke. I sat there more frustrated trying to figure out why things are not working in the AI bottleneck system. Wearhouse overfull of materials that are not being moved to product line. I tried every thing. Demo and rebuilds, exit and reloads, pause and un pause production. Overemployed my wear house. every suggestion. I spent 22 hrs of my life I will never get back on a dead end. They are still waiting and praying instead of walking next door literally, to get supplies to make Amor.  I would love to play this game, but the AI bottleneck that is happening is causing a real bad taste. This game I know has bugs, but come on. its been a year. This particular problem should have been sorted  out by now. Just saying. It's the basic mechanics of the game. Resource, produce, sell or use. That system is broke and has been, and clearly trying the game again since launch shows that there has been no effort to fix that problem. I should not have to look for ways to work around the bug.
It would be easy to start an actual village with Medieval technology, than figure out this game's broken mechanics and terrible UI.

its a good game, its fun and easy to play and spend hours on but after that theres no real progression and it gets quite boring, after your first town hits its peak you dont really need any incentive to build another city. overall a fun game but needs more features and progression. i wouldnt buy it in its current state but it looks very promising!

Not updated frequently enough which affects replayability. Though when the new updates DO drop the game is very fun!.. but that doesn't change the fact that the game is effectively still the same as it was in 2024, which doesn't bode well for the future. The random apologies and sporadic updates just isn't professional enough.

The game itself is fun and immersive, there just isn't enough replayability. I have no clue how people are racking up 100s of hours and are still having fun.

Some things worth adding:
- Creative mode. Then making castles and towns is easy. A lot of the appeal for this game is visual.
- Multiplayer PvP or co-op PvE
- proper post-game
- post-game summaries

TL;DR up top: Reasonably solid bones for a city builder that is still in development, so buyer beware. Probably a pass for the average player (at least until 1.0) but a must-have for the Venn diagram overlap of fans of central European late medieval history and fans of city builder games.

Good town builder with some not so good combat. Most notable for its attention to detail in depicting the late middle ages in central Europe. As always, early access means one should be hesitant to purchase. At 40 USD the price is steep, so to be clear up front: This game has been in early access for a long time, and will likely continue to have a slow development cycle for its whole life. The developer seems reliable, but works slowly. Watch some Youtube videos, decide if it's your thing, it's not a game that I would recommend to all my friends, but for the specific subset of people who really like late medieval history and really like city builders, it's a good time.

The strong parts of this game are largely related to its dedication to the historical setting: The burgage plot system is a great gameplay system that does a reasonable job of modeling medieval economies and social structure, and encourages building a village in a way that looks reasonably believable to a layman (even if few players villages will actually look like real medieval communities in actuality). Right now, the game is mostly the early-to-mid game, while the late game is still getting implemented. Big towns get a little boring at the moment as once they're established, there's not too much left to do. I think the implementation of city specializations will help this a bit as forcing the user to specialize the regions they control will likely bring a bit more mid-to-late game planning. The development of the combat system will also likely help the late game.

On that topic, the combat is probably the place where this game has the most to grow. It feels clunky and is very easy to cheese. I think this is mostly because the developer knows that the city building and economy are the core systems, and so the combat is necessarily at a lower level of implementation at time of writing. I would love to see them put just as much care into the combat system as the city building system eventually, and make a game that goes farther than the Total War formula and actually represents medieval warfare in a realistic way, but whether that will happen remains to be seen. If they can achieve the same depth of historical believability in combat as they do in city building, that would be truly special, but just getting it to MTW2 levels would be enough to satisfy me. As is, it's not quite there but is serviceable for this stage of development.

I do worry a bit that the game will never see completion, as it's been in development for a long time, but if it keeps trudging along and eventually gets to 1.0 with the same level of quality that the most complete parts of the game currently have, I think it'll be a genuinely great game.

There is a lot of potential here but this game is very much alpha with a lot of development needed before you could do some form of play through.

Positives: great graphics, embroyonic growth with freedom to design your village. The mix of city builder with castle defense is a nice addition. Development tree, whilst 20% complete, hints at some nice specialiisation paths to take.

Cons: lots of bugs (stuck livestock, archers don't man towers/walls, farming system clunky causing failed harvests easily), lack of detail in the UI (try to figure out what a small-holding specialises in easily, status of trading buildings) and lack of depth (regions are treated as separate entities, villages and roads never graphically evolve into towns and paved roads.

Probably the most boring and barebones city builder type of game I've ever played. It's got basically no depth, and like most Early Access slop it's charging a premium price tag for what's basically a tech demo with some gameplay. If there wasn't a janky combat layer tacked on to pressure you, you'd basically experience everything the game has to offer in 30 minutes.

Have no idea if most of these positive reviews are botted or something because you could just play Tropico and have a much more fleshed out version of this, complete with it's own janky and tacked on combat. There's no advisors, no real interaction, just the same plodding tracks of lute music and waiting forever for harvests and the material to build more plots.

Presentation wise there's basically nothing to it. Some peasant voicelines(And I only mean some; Prepare to hear the same line about feeding month old grain to an Ox over and over.), a few pop ups of static images to vaguely convey some nothingburger event, and anything remotely interesting and impactful having a 'COMING SOON!' sticker plastered on it even though this game has been in EA for two years.

I feel like the dev couldn't decide if he wanted to make an RTS or a city builder, so they tried both and made both aspects boring and lifeless. At the end of the day it's just more scammy early access crap that peddles an interesting idea, charges you a high dollar point with vague promises and timelines, then just hands you a cardboard box with the rudimentary framework of whatever game you paid for after having a bunch of youtubers tell you how great the game supposedly is.

Months passed since I bought the game, still same bugs, still no progress in development. I guess Steam has to introduce a policy with commitment for developers who release half baked stuff. Cause it looks like supporting an emerging developer but in practice translates into never being able to play the game, and having to live with limitations of early access forever with no lead time.. It will be a nice game ONCE and IF will be ever completed and problems solved. Same publisher has a dozen of games "in progress" i don't see any progress in this one.
To be constructive : 
- clothing bug is still there after 4 months, tailors never bring ready clothes to the storehouse, so it just sits at tailor's and disappears into thin air (guess double bug). Can't upgrade the housings because of that, Issue Level - Game breaking
- perks for advancing city level - still in progress after 4 months, at the same exact level of completion
- Experience for retinue - same no visible progress or new stuff 
- Taverns consume ale as it was air, not game breaking but very annoying.

Morale - i don't recommend purchasing this game NOW. I really gave it a few months thinking i will be back later and will see new added things, not the case, NOTHING new added. I repeat it is a very immersive game that I like, and once and if it will be completed then it will be a gem

Got this on sale and seemed like a good deal at the time. I was able to quickly build a successful settlement, then I just kind of ran out of things to do. The trailer and description implies a much deeper, more complex game than what is available.

The bones for a great game are here, but the way this is being presented to the public is deceptive. For the amount of copes sold and the amount of hype around this game, I would consider it a disappointment at this juncture. Not a bad game, just not what we were expecting. 

I am hoping that more updates will release more content, but I am skeptical given how incomplete this game is after several years in development. I will happily change my rating if that is the case, but at this point, I would only recommend this game if you find it in the bargain bin. 

P.S.: I have so many hours logged because I stepped away from my computer and forgot to come back and shut it down for over 24hr hours. I probably logged 25 hours on the game, so I did give it an honest attempt.

I don't often play these types of games, the last one I played that's similar to this was cities skylines 2. (At least I think it is)
So I wanted to try this one.
Been struggling just to learn the game mechanics, the tutorial doesn't really help that much, that I had to look up youtube just to understand how to play it.

leaving a thumbs up, because still generally neutral about it, I like the idea of the game, but the game could do a better job explaining how to play it. (Or maybe it's just me.)
Great builder and management game, best i've seen lately and the devs are going in the right way.

It's not that terrible. But also not that good. Just got really stale and really boring really quickly. Seriously not worth anywhere near the regular price.

It feels like one of those games with the disheveled box you'd find in the 5 dollar bin. Remember those? Yeah, actually a lot of those titles are still probably better than this one. 🤷‍♂️

TLDR: My real verdict is "it depends."  First: the game is beautiful; it is also extremely system intensive.  Second: The game is unfinished; expect bugs galore and little balance.  Third: For many people, like myself, the game was not what I thought it was: it is just a medieval city builder.  If you are just looking for a city builder but set in medieval times, then you are going to have a good time.  If the ads and opening vids made you think that this is like total war but with city building attached because you crave a new Medieval Total War, be forewarned: this is not that.

Longer version:

If you are looking for a medieval city builder where you will spend most of your time designing burgage plots (aka: houses with veggies attached) and watching oxen drag lumber around: then this is the game for you.  

If, however, you were -- like me -- drawn in by the videos and ads that made this look more like a total war kind of game with a city builder as a secondary feature... you will be sorely disappointed.  If you were expecting something challenging with a clean UI, maybe a Medieval Tropico... or maybe you were expecting a Crusader Kings city builder (something with a lot of character roleplaying)... again, you will be... disappointed.  

Manor Lords is simply a medieval city builder where most of the player base spends there time discussing how to break the economy by spamming sheep, veggie plots, or weapons (not for fighting - but for selling).

I am happy to support indie devs and the game is impressive for basically being made by just one guy.... But it is just not for me.  I wish it was, but I've played Banished, Tropico, RimWorld, Against the Storm, and countless other city builders that offered similar things.  I was looking for something fresh or challenging and I, sadly, did not find what I was looking for.  While I could spend time building a large settlement... why would I?  I've done this before.

I wish the game started with a full developed town and that the game itself was maintaining power and playing politics.  You know... like a MANOR LORD.  But hey, if you've never played Banished or RimWorld or Tropico before, then this will all seem completely new to you and you will have a great time.

Devs asked for review, so here it is. 

Whenever I play this game, I love it. I did not spend here crazy amount of time, but the game catches me very well. With recent upate I felt like its a completely new game and its kind of hard to get into the rythm of nonstop producing tools. 
But I am losing the track of updates. I am not playing it on daily basis. Its more like a chill out session.
But this game is kind of addictive. I find myself lack of self control playing this game, searching for optimization, creating new areas, planing ahead, dealing with bandits or raiders.. There is so much to do.. 
I need to take a look at farming system right now since last time automated version was pretty inefficient. 
But I like the game. The vibes are real, you wont regret buying it. 

I am just waiting for full release. But I appreciate a lot of work done by devs here and focusing on systems first. I hope, the perks will be good enough so it will be even more fun. 

A good recommendation for any player considering to support these small studios. Take your time with the game. Try to optimize by yourself and struggle. Do not go for quick guides on youtube, since its corrupting your game experience. I was doing it at the start and then I didnt want to play it, because I have seen it all. 
After the big upgrade, hell yeah, what a ride.

I can't leave the main menu to start the game. The music is just too good.

10/10

The resource system is great, but devs really need to work on the balance for rival lords/bandits mass producing combat units. It's the medieval era and my town has a dozen families, but apparently its worth hundreds of lives of able bodied men? Every year? The time dilation is ridiculous too. It takes 5 families a month to build one small house because they're waiting on the ox. Mid-late game gets pretty boring as well. The game tries too hard to sell the "realism" of the poorly optimized mechanics.
Absolute trashcan bugs in castle planner. Can't even see cancel or commit buttons. Makes it unusable, which means no point in playing the game as it's a critical building.

TLDR: It's pretty to look at for a City Builder, but still feels very unfinished and lacking in a way for players new to the genre to have fun without feeling overwhelmed inside the refund window. Can't recommend, except to genre veterans atm. 

It was nice too look at for a city builder graphically, but I got bored an hour in, and it drops you right into the gameplay. I don't personally care for games like this that don't offer the player any motivation other than letting you build a city you can manage. It certainly feels like another city builder, but not on a grid, and with some quirky new ways to design roads and plots. I just found it lacking in character, the (sort of ) tutorial is just dialogue boxes you click until you've built each kind of thing, and you can easily get stuck wondering what you're supposed to do next, as it's not at all easy to just jump into and start playing. I would prefer this to be how the game is after some kind of campaign that helps teach you how to play and why certain buildings are important. As it is, it feels better left to the genre vets who may find the controls, and what to do, to be second nature already. I see the potential for hundreds of hours of fun with the creativity allowed here, but not personally if you're gonna make me spend as many hours on youtube looking up how or what to do.

whenever i enter combat with a opposing town my troops do the complete opposite which resulted in my town getting attacked multiple times after all my troops were watching my other squadron get wiped out and they just sat there this has happened a lot of times now i will not be re installing it until its fixed or the game is fully out i know its made by one person but the price is ridiculous for a game this buggy not worth my money
Game needs something like direct join, host-client type multiplayer for co-op type play. No games have this anymore and it's a shame.

It has some good things going but, despite playing it again after quite a while to wait for updates, it's still not that deep for this genre's standards. Once the novelty of the first 5 or so hours wears off, you'll already be close to as deep as it gets. Those first couple hours are fun, but beyond that you'll run into more and more unfinished features. Given the amount of time that passed since I last played, I expected more. Can't recommend it if you're looking for more than a couple hours of fun.
The game has somehow gotten worse from when I first played it, which led to refunding. Now I'm 4 hours in and past my window, what the flying hell have these devs been doing?
Super fun for what its worth.  Can't wait for the full release!  I will say though, I do miss the old upgrade system, but I look forward to seeing the direction it heads to for full release.

It was pretty good and rounded on the early access release, but honestly now its just a micro managment hellhole with tedious new mechanics. Honestly it felt like in the 2 years ince Ea Release the game took a step back.

Also who decided that it is fun having to barter between regions, i mean for those who want sure, but for most players its just tedious and leads to even more annoying micro that got worse with the updates.

I really still hope that they will eventually fix it. But it has been such a long time already.

existing content is great, but the lack of updates is very annoying and very little is added each "major update" on top of this much of the game has coming soon labels for things that are supposed to play a pretty big role in the game. My biggest complaint is the castle building which is in my opinion is the peak of a playthrough but the system is straight buns if you want to build walls you need to make it continuous or else they don't line up and the walls form curves instead of right angles on top of that walls only connect to buildings on two points this makes you lose the ability to make new shapes for castles. General gameplay can get annoying due to the resource collection systems where if you drain a deposit you have to import everything which costs so much money and I get that it could be in place to make the game more difficult but it gets to a point where its just tedious trying to balance your economy. Another thing that's bad about the game is the adversary's the on map ones are not very smart and if you want to conquer anything they bring out a giant army even if you have enough people heavy losses can set you way back. All in all the games content is alright but systems can get tedious and the long gaps between updates just for there to be one or two new things stops it from reaching its full potential.

For me the game falls in the middle leaning more towards the negative side. Final thought get it when its on sale or not at all

I love this game, but like some of the recent reviews, the development of this game is awfully slow. Not to mention that during all these years of development, the devs haven't even bothered to make a workshop page on steam and make the game better for mods to run. If official development is going to be at a snail's pace, they should add official modding support into their games so that players aren't stuck with playing the same things over and over again.
disjointed updates, tedious gameplay. there are better building sims out there.

Really want this game to be good, however after owning it 2 + years its still really half baked. I'm all for early access and indie devs but this trend of releasing stuff that's less than half complete is just nonsense and frankly I'm taking stand across the board on these games. The consumer isn't here to "finish" your game for you or get endlessly pulled along over years and years "development" . Valve needs to set to a policy and get these devs to commit to a timeline or just honestly have them piss off.
Potential to be one of the best Castle Simulator/Sandbox games out there. Devs are great with feedback and is a solid all around game to waste plenty of hours on

Great Game seriously you guys have done great work, everything so far is absolutely amazing. NPC's pathfinding is absolutely retarded though, it has ruined every single one of my playthroughs and I've constantly been trying to work around it every new game. It's usually the butchers or just any enterable building used for some sort of production, also the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ rivers, why do they have such a hard time crossing the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ rivers, the oxen sometimes wanna make me blow my brains out with the way they get stuck sometimes too. Anyways Greg i hope your reading this i love you and your team im looking forward to seeing this games future development and to anyone reading this its definantly worth the buy, even if the game is slavjunk right now every update is usually a big fix def worth thanks bye see ya.

The game has been out for over 2 years now, and it looks like it became one of those ''early acess'' forever games. 

I played this a lot since the start, and honestly all the ''major updates'' are just tiny improvements and bug fixes. We got new maps and thats about it. The game is very shallow and has no real deep meaningful mechanics. 

Thats why i cant recommend this game, because the gameplay loop is very limited, and in over 2 years theres barley any new meaningful content. I have really tried to be supportive of this game but at the end of the day if development cant happen at a reasonable rate then its not gonna work out because people are going to loose interest if a game is in early acess for years and years and there is no meanigful content added.
Would love to recommend, but the dev allows insanely annoying game breaking glitches to go unpatched for years on end.  Either hire more people or fix what's wrong.

This game can be clunky and full of bugs, and sometimes is so chill that its almost dull if you're not in that kind of mood. It's got some cool systems and looks nice. Starting to have fun with it now just building up my city.
Unplayable slow performance on my laptop that runs other similar games fine, even on minimum graphics settings. Don't buy if you're on an integrated graphics card.

Not worth the Cost imo,
Starting out i really liked it, its nice and chill and getting to understand the mechanics.
once i figured all that out and started a new round, thats when ♥♥♥♥ hit the fan.
farmers constantly "waiting" during the seasons they should be plowing or sowing, even after i remove the families and then add them again they work for about 5 seconds then get back to "waiting"
oh you want to contest some land, no worries i pay for some mercs go to the battlefield, fight starts, we charge eachother. then we all just hang out and look at eachother and no1 is being killed.
save game enter it again 1min of killing then bros hanging out.

Only worth it on sale. Full price $40 for effectively a third of a game that's gotten less updates in over a year of early access than TF2. What's there so far is cool and fun but really needs any level of transparency on development.
This game is much better than most of the “copy-paste” games in the similar genre. It’s very unique and satisfying. Even for an EA game, it’s already worth getting.

Can sink hours and hours into game, in the beginning don't worry too much about fast forwarding as you'll want to get the mechanics and fundamentals down. Also don't overbuild housing too soon without getting food production up there.

beautiful game. I got lost in building my town until all the people started starving. I think that the core economics of the game are spectacular and the way you grow your town is quite intuitive, including the trade. I just loved it and I wanted to grow my city more and more. It was a challenge to understand the economics so my village could keep growing.

I don't however, understand the combat. When my people started starving i just panicked and sent a whole lot of soldiers to invade another region, but i couldn't attack anyone or anything, aside from bandits. Is that because i'm already 'warring' with them? Mind you, i think it's on the right track in terms of how you can administer tactics, i like it very much.I gotta say, i also enjoyed not being attacked by my next door neighbours, maybe that's because i set up the campaign that way, but it was relaxing.

I also didn't see much in the way of diplomacy, can't comment much on this.

The game crashed wehen i opted to look at my village in 3rd person.

Excited to see this game finished, fantasic work so far

This game is parallel to 7 Days to Die for me.

one of the best city builders ive ever played with the most fun and engaging city defense mechanic ive seen in a game, but the game does the same thing that turns me off to most city builders that let you expand into other provinces, when you take another province instead of just incorporating it into your already bult up city it treats it as a fresh start where you have to build up from square 1, you can move resources from your first settlement to the new one but i find that a tedious and kills alot of the fun for me, i hope in the future when you take another province it will just allow you to build and pull from your main settlement without the need of trading posts

Manor Lords shines with its detailed portrayal of daily village life and immersive atmosphere, yet the process of moving goods between regions feels unnecessarily complicated and would benefit from a more streamlined approach. Performance is generally well-optimized, though occasional delays in resource transfers or task execution interrupt the rhythm of play. With smoother logistics and reduced lag, the game’s strong design could reach an even higher level of satisfaction.
Wonderfully pleasant and picturesque. I love looking at my bustling little village and noticing what a labor of love this game is. Really excited to see more developments.
I'm a big fan of how intricate the ecosystem in this game is. like how realistic the societal needs are, and how all the people function!
for some reason it crashed on me now when i boot it up its crashes over and over

Well, I bought this yesterday and it kicked my ass the first play but once I got used to how it works, which is a fair bit different than other builders, (in a good way), I started a new village and I can honestly say that this game is better than most builder games that are supposedly finished!

Grats on a great game Tim, been holding off buying it for a while but glad I now have. Keep up the good work!

Beautifully crafted, I have watched several videos and played this game as well. You are free to design your town as you please. I really enjoy the creative aspects of this game. Highly recommend!

i didn't encounter any bugs, lots of fun. do research on how to do everything. resource management is interesting, still trying to completely figure it out. more tutorials would be very helpful with trading, best ways to build a town. all that sort of stuff. will be playing this for a long time. i have the settings on high and its beautiful.

this game is the nest RTS game i have ever played and there is nothing id want more than to enjoy it with some friends or have more of a challenge when playing the game the only thing i feel that needs work are the other AI regions once they get to a certain point their progress just seems locked and they villages dont grow anymore

I´ll have to give Manor Lords a recommendation, though with some pause. 
The good: 
- The graphics and "feeling" of the game are outstanding
- You population (AI) mostly* does sensible things (rare in a city builder)
- The burgage plot system is fun and ads a nice dimension to this sort of game. 
- The balance between city builder and RTS is good, you use your army from time to time, but mostly build. 
- The flexibility of building plots create very good looking villages/cities. 

The bad:
- The farming system is very frustrating and you need to micro manage it to death, with often pretty meagre results in exchange for the heavy work load. 
- Upgrading houses feels more like a handicap than something that gives you the great benefits it should. 
- Your opponents army AI is simply broken. Pathfinding is just not there.  
- Taking new areas is not really needed, and the reward for doing so is too small (basically getting one additional retinue, since you don´t need money in mid/end game). If you could freely send recourses to your main area, with out the barter system, they might be more useful and/or if you could make more army from these areas. 
- Not going to go into things that are not finished,  castle/manor system etc. 
-Currently the villagers get buggy and get stuck when your city gets to big, so late/late game is not really working.

One of the best looking, and atmospheric city builders out there, with a potential for a fun RTS element. Still not finished, and the development is slow, but hopefully will continue.
